sourceDomain.name: CPU Cache
sourceDomain.description: L1/L2/L3 cache hierarchy in modern CPUs — small, fast memory sitting between registers and RAM

entities.name: Cache Miss
entities.description: Requested data not in cache — must fetch from slower memory

entities.name: Eviction Policy
entities.description: LRU — the least-recently-used entry is evicted when cache is full

entities.name: Cache Hit Rate
entities.description: Fraction of requests served from cache; high rate means low latency

entities.name: Cache Line
entities.description: Fixed-size block fetched atomically; locality of reference boosts efficiency

relations.description: A miss may trigger eviction if space must be made

targetDomain.name: Application Cache Layer
targetDomain.description: Redis/Memcached layer serving a web application — sits between app servers and the primary database

entities.name: Cache Miss
entities.description: Key not found in Redis — application falls through to the database

entities.name: TTL / Eviction Policy
entities.description: maxmemory-policy (allkeys-lru) — expired or least-recently-used keys evicted on memory pressure

entities.name: Hit Rate
entities.description: Percentage of requests answered from cache without hitting the DB

entities.name: Cache Granularity
entities.description: Size of cached objects; over-large objects waste memory; too-small objects cause excess chatter

relations.description: Cache miss under memory pressure triggers eviction of LRU entries

mappings.justification: Both represent the failure to find the requested item in fast storage, forcing a slower retrieval
Both use an LRU-based policy to decide which data to discard when capacity is exhausted
Hit rate is the primary performance indicator in both systems — high rate = low latency overhead
Both have a fundamental unit of data transfer; locality-of-reference benefits translate across domains

insights.description: Warming the cache after deployment is the app-layer equivalent of a CPU cache cold-start after power-on
insights.sourceEvidence: CPU caches start cold; the first round of instructions pays full memory-access penalty
insights.targetApplication: Plan for a warm-up period after Redis restarts; route a pre-heat script before live traffic

insights.description: Caching objects larger than optimal granularity wastes capacity just as large cache lines can waste CPU bandwidth
insights.sourceEvidence: Cache-line pollution is a known CPU performance pitfall when accessing sparse fields of a large struct
insights.targetApplication: Store fine-grained cache keys (per-entity) rather than serializing entire result sets unless they are always accessed together

inferences.sourcePattern: Prefetching adjacent cache lines improves throughput when access is sequential
inferences.targetPrediction: Pre-warming related keys (e.g., the next page of paginated results) should improve P99 latency for browsing users

limitations: CPU caches are hardware-managed and deterministic; Redis eviction is heuristic and approximate
CPU cache size is fixed at chip design time; Redis can be scaled horizontally — the analogy breaks down for capacity planning
Cache coherence across multiple CPUs has no clean analogue in single-node Redis; distributed caching needs a separate model
